subject: LU-8826 recovery: don't shrink recovery hard time

values.-1: I would prefer that you didn't submit this
values.+1: Looks good to me, but someone else must approve

Patch Set 1: Code-Review-1

I think massive umount can become very slow, especially with ZFS.

Patch Set 1:

I agree with you, but that's just kind of compromise. If we don't do that synchronously, one umount client could make whole recovery failed (all clients evicted), and result in data loss. Given that massive umount can be well scheduled & controlled by admin, I think the severity is lower than data loss caused by failed recovery. What do you think?

Patch Set 1:

this hasn't been an issue for years, AFAIU. plus we've got VBR and in the majority of cases a missing client should not cause many evictions.

Patch Set 1:

hmm, look at the check_for_recovery_ready(), we still wait for all recoverable clients connect, is it a vbr bug? Or did I miss anything?

Patch Set 1:

yes, we do wait to ensure every alive client has its chance, but we don't abort immediately on a gap.

Patch Set 1:

indeed, I'll look closer to see why all clients got evicted in test case. Thank you.

Patch Set 1:

It turns out that client evictions are caused by we extend recovery time, but didn't extend recovery hard time, so once recovery time expired, recovery hard time expired at the same time. I think we need to extend recovery hard time along with recovery time. Will update the ticket description and patch.

Patch Set 1: Verified-1

Tests uploaded by Onyx Autotest from onyx-37vm1 for review-dne-part-1 received by maloo, run on Red Hat Enterprise Linux Server 7.3/x86_64: (https://testing.hpdd.intel.com/test_sessions/7b4a98e2-a860-11e6-bd69-5254006e85c2). Ran 7 tests. No failures.

Patch Set 1:

Recovery "hard" limit is supposed to be exactly that - the hard limit of how long recovery can take, and it shouldn't be extended.  That said, if the current default value is too low then we can change this.

Do you know why the recovery was taking so long?  Is it because there were many 10s of thousands of RPCs to be replayed/resent, or is there something else making the recovery slow (e.g. every recovery request is sync'd to disk?

Patch Set 1:

PPC64 Builder: build succesful

Patch Set 1:

Andreas, the recovery took so long because the server is waiting for a client that'll never connect. I think if we can extend 'recovery time', then we must extend 'hard time' to make sure it's greater than 'recovery time', so that we can try VBR after 'recovery time' expired  and before 'hard time' reached, otherwise, VBR won't be able to take affection and any dead client can make the whole recovery failed.

Patch Set 1:

BTW: since IR introduced, both default 'recovery time' & 'hard time' can be very small, in this test case, default 'recovery time' is 60s, 'hard time' is 90s, and when recovery start, 'recovery time' is extended to 90s (same as 'hard time').

Patch Set 1:

Probably it was a mistake to shrink 'hard timer' for IR?

Patch Set 1:

Xiong, what's your opinion? Probably we shouldn't shrink 'hard timer' for IR, and we'd make sure 'soft < hard' in first recovery stage (waiting or all clients connections)?

parents.subject: LU-8793 tests: add version check to conf-sanity.sh test_99

commit.subject: LU-8826 recovery: don't shrink recovery hard time
commit.message:
LU-8826 recovery: don't shrink recovery hard time

1. Don't shrink recovery hard time, otherwise, soft limit will be
   very close to hard limit and hard timer can be expired easily
   during the 1st recovery stage: waiting for all clients
   connected, and VBR won't take effect at all. Added replay-vbr
   test_13 to verify this.
2. When exp_need_sync is set, all transactions on MDT/OST should
   be synchronous.
3. Removed unused target_client_add_cb().

Signed-off-by: Niu Yawei <yawei.niu@intel.com>
Change-Id: Idbb011f2772d5c779c53c3e990ceef5a386e4b5b
Reviewed-on: https://review.whamcloud.com/23716
Tested-by: Jenkins
Reviewed-by: Lai Siyao <lai.siyao@intel.com>
Tested-by: Maloo <hpdd-maloo@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Alex Zhuravlev <alexey.zhuravlev@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Oleg Drokin <oleg.drokin@intel.com>
